name: Why are there so many models?
tagline: GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and a dozen version numbers — the list looks enormous and the pattern behind it is tiny. A model is one specific trained AI brain; a few companies each build their own, each ships a few sizes, and each keeps releasing new versions. A few makers times a few sizes times versions. That's the whole map, and you'll never have to memorise it.
overview: The confusion this module kills is the wall of names — "GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama… I'm completely lost." It is the least technical possible way to make the AI landscape stop feeling scary. A model is one specific trained AI brain: the thing that actually reads your words and writes a reply. Different companies build their own — OpenAI makes the GPT family, Anthropic makes Claude, Google makes Gemini, Meta makes Llama — and that's the first source of names. The second is that each maker ships several sizes of its brain: a big flagship that's the smartest but slower and pricier to run, and smaller ones that are faster and cheaper and plenty for simple jobs. The third is time: makers keep releasing newer versions as they improve, so the numbers pile up. Multiply those three and the terrifying list resolves into something small — a few makers, times a few sizes, times versions over time. Carry one everyday picture the whole way through: car manufacturers. Different companies (Toyota, Ford, VW), each with a small city car and a big estate, each bringing out new model years — and you have never needed to know every trim to pick a car that fits your journey. This module deliberately does NOT crown a winner: "which model is best for me" is a real question with a real answer, and it's the next module's job, not this one's. What you get here is a map and a three-question filter that turns the endless list into a two-line shortlist — and permission to change your mind whenever you like, because picking a model is a Tuesday decision, not a marriage.
objectives: Explain in one plain sentence what an AI model is — one specific trained AI brain — and why the long list of names exists: a few makers each build their own, each ships several sizes, and each keeps releasing newer versions.

Name the real model families honestly (OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, Meta's Llama) without treating any one as the winner — knowing that "which is best for me" is a separate question for the next module.

Use the three-question filter — is it big or small, is it recent, does it fit my job — to turn the overwhelming list into a shortlist, and recognise the three traps: assuming a higher version number beats a rival maker, assuming bigger is always better, and assuming you must pick once and forever.

lessons.title: The map: makers, sizes, and versions
lessons.objective: Understand what an AI model actually is, and see the scary list of names resolve into a tiny pattern — a few makers, each shipping a few sizes, each releasing new versions over time.
lessons.body:
## The list looks huge. The pattern behind it is tiny.

If you've spent ten minutes reading about AI, you've been buried in names: GPT-this, Claude-that, Gemini, Llama, and version numbers stacked on top of every one of them. It feels like a field you'd need a month to even *start* understanding.

Here's the reassuring truth up front: **the list is long, but the pattern that generates it is tiny.** Once you see the pattern, the names stop being a wall and become a menu. This lesson hands you the pattern. It has exactly three parts.

## First, what is a "model," really?

Before the names, the word. A **model** is one specific trained AI brain — the actual thing that reads what you type and writes a reply. When someone says "which model are you using," they mean *which one of these trained brains* is doing the work.

In the last module you met the idea that AI is a system that learned patterns from an enormous amount of text. A *model* is one finished, packaged result of that training — one brain, with its own name, that a company built and now offers for people to use. That's all the word means. Not a brand, not an app, not a subscription: **one trained brain.**

So the whole question of this module is really just: *why are there so many trained brains, and how do I not drown in them?* Three reasons, coming up.

## Reason one: different companies each build their own

Several companies build these brains, and each builds its own family of them. You'll see the same handful of names everywhere, so here they are, plainly and neutrally:

- **OpenAI** makes the family called **GPT**.
- **Anthropic** makes the family called **Claude**.
- **Google** makes the family called **Gemini**.
- **Meta** (the Facebook company) makes the family called **Llama**.

There are others, but those four are the ones you'll bump into again and again. Notice the shape: a **company** builds a **family**, and the family has a **name**. "GPT" is not a product you buy so much as OpenAI's line of brains, the way a car maker has a line of cars. That's the first source of all those names — several makers, each with their own family. No ranking here on purpose; we'll come back to why in the next lesson.

## Reason two: each maker ships several sizes

Here's the part most people miss, and it clears up half the confusion on its own. Each maker doesn't build *one* brain — it builds a **range of sizes**.

- A **big** one — the flagship. It's the smartest, handles the hardest thinking, and in return it's **slower and more expensive to run**.
- **Smaller** ones — faster, cheaper, and honestly *plenty* for everyday jobs: quick replies, simple rewrites, routine questions.

That's why one maker seems to have so many names by itself. It's not five unrelated products — it's the **same family in different sizes**, the way a coffee shop sells the same coffee in small, medium, and large. Big brain for the hard problem; small brain for the quick one. You'll learn in a moment that reaching for the biggest one every time is a beginner reflex worth dropping — often the small one is the *right* tool, not a compromise.

## Reason three: they keep releasing new versions

The third source of names is simply **time**. Makers keep improving their brains and releasing **newer versions**, and the older ones don't always vanish right away. So the version numbers stack up: this year's is generally better than last year's from the *same* maker, and next year's will be better still.

That's genuinely all a version number tells you: **newer, from that same company, usually an improvement.** It does not let you compare *across* makers — a bigger number from one company doesn't mean it beats a different company's brain. (That trap gets its own warning in the next lesson, because almost everyone falls into it once.)

## Put the three together: the whole map

Now multiply them. **A few makers, times a few sizes each, times new versions over time.** That's it. That is the entire reason the list looks the way it does — not chaos, just three small things multiplied:

> makers × sizes × versions = the big scary list

Read the list back through that lens and it calms right down. "A big recent Claude," "a small fast GPT," "last year's Gemini" — every name you'll ever see is just *some maker, some size, some version.* You don't memorise the list. You read it.

## The picture to carry: car manufacturers

Hold one everyday image for the rest of this module, because it maps perfectly.

Think about **cars**. There are several **manufacturers** — Toyota, Ford, Volkswagen — and that's your *makers*. Each one sells a **range**: a little city car for nipping around town, a big estate for hauling the family and the luggage — that's your *sizes*, small-and-thrifty versus big-and-capable. And every year or two they release a **new model year** with improvements — that's your *versions*.

Here's the part that matters most: **you have never needed to know every manufacturer, every model, and every trim level to pick a car that gets you to work.** You think about your journey and you choose something that fits. Nobody is expected to have the whole catalogue memorised, and nobody sensible feels stupid for that. AI models are exactly the same. A few makers, a few sizes, always a newer model year — and a simple way to pick, which is the whole of the next lesson.

knowledgeCheck.stem: In plain terms, what is an AI "model"?

options.text: One specific trained AI brain — the actual thing that reads what you type and writes a reply
A monthly subscription plan you buy to get access to AI
The company that builds AI, such as OpenAI or Google
The app or website you open to chat with an AI

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A model is one finished, trained AI brain with its own name — the thing doing the reading and writing. It isn't a subscription (b): that's how you might pay for access, not the brain itself. It isn't the company (c): OpenAI and Google are the makers that *build* models, not the models. And it isn't the app or site (d): that's just the door you walk through to reach a model. Keeping "model = one trained brain" straight is what makes the rest of the list readable.

knowledgeCheck.stem: The module says the huge list of model names comes from three things multiplied together. What are they?

options.text: A few makers, times several sizes each, times new versions released over time
Price, speed, and the colour of the app's logo
The number of countries a model is sold in, times languages, times users
One giant model per company, renamed slightly for marketing each week

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Makers × sizes × versions is the whole pattern: several companies each build a family, each family comes in a range of sizes, and each keeps releasing newer versions — multiply those and you get the long list. Price, speed and logos (b) are things you might *compare*, not what generates the names. Countries, languages and users (c) aren't why the model list is long. And it isn't one model renamed for marketing (d) — the different sizes and versions are genuinely different brains, like a car maker's real range, not a rebrand.

lessons.title: The three-question filter (and the three traps)
lessons.objective: Turn the whole model list into a shortlist using three plain questions — big or small, recent, fits the job — and sidestep the three beginner traps, without needing anyone to crown a single best model.
lessons.body:
## From a wall of names to a shortlist

The last lesson gave you the map: makers × sizes × versions. This one gives you the compass — a way to *use* the map without memorising it. When you're staring at a list of models and someone asks which to pick, you only ever need **three questions.** Ask them in order and the endless list collapses into a shortlist you can actually choose from.

## Question 1: is it big or small?

This is the smart-and-costly versus fast-and-cheap choice from the last lesson, turned into a decision. Ask: **does this job actually need the big brain?**

- **Hard thinking** — a tricky analysis, careful reasoning, a delicate piece of writing — reach for a **big** model. That's what the flagship is for.
- **Simple, high-volume, or quick** — a short reply, a straightforward rewrite, a routine question — a **small** model is faster, cheaper, and completely up to it.

The instinct to always grab the biggest one is worth resisting. It's like hiring a top surgeon to put on a plaster: overkill, slower to book, and it costs more for no better result. Matching the size to the job *is* the skill.

## Question 2: is it recent?

All else being equal, a **newer version from the same maker is usually better** than an older one — that's why makers keep releasing them. So when you're choosing between two versions of the same family, lean newer unless you've got a specific reason not to.

The trap lurking here — flagged properly below — is thinking a version number lets you compare *across different makers*. It doesn't. "Recent" is a tie-breaker *within* a family (this year's Claude versus last year's Claude), not a scoreboard *between* families.

## Question 3: does it fit my job?

The last question is the most human and the most important: **does this model suit the actual thing I'm trying to do?** Some jobs care about cost above all; some need the very best reasoning and will pay for it; some need speed; some need a model that's especially good with images or code. "Fit" just means *matching the tool to the task in front of you* rather than chasing whichever name is loudest this week.

And here's the freeing part: **you don't have to get this perfect, and you're not locked in.** Try one, see how it does on your real work, switch if it disappoints. Which brings us to the biggest relief in this whole module — but first, the three traps, because almost everyone hits at least one.

## Trap 1: "a higher version number must beat a rival"

You'll see something like "version 4" from one maker and "version 2" from another and assume the bigger number wins. It doesn't work that way. **Version numbers only compare a maker to its own past** — they're each company counting its own releases, on its own private clock. One company's "4" and another's "2" are just two different families' internal counters; the number tells you nothing about which brain is better across the fence. Comparing *across* makers is a real question — and it's the next module's entire job, done honestly.

## Trap 2: "bigger is always better"

The flagship is the smartest, so surely you should always use it? No. **Bigger costs more and runs slower, and for most everyday jobs the small model is not a compromise — it's the right choice.** Using the giant model for a one-line reply is pure waste: you wait longer and pay more for an answer the small one would have nailed. Reserve the big brain for the genuinely hard problems. This is the plaster-and-surgeon point again, because it's the trap people fall into most.

## Trap 3: "I have to pick one and commit forever"

This is the one that causes needless stress, so let it go now. **Choosing a model is a Tuesday decision, not a marriage.** You can change your mind any time — next week, next hour, mid-task. Nothing about trying one model stops you trying another. People agonise over the "right" pick as though it's permanent and expensive; it's neither. Pick a reasonable one, do your work, and switch the moment something serves you better.

## The whole map, in one breath

That's the module. **A few makers, a few sizes, always improving** — and three questions (big or small, recent, fits the job) that turn the scary list into a shortlist. Notice what this lesson carefully did *not* do: it never told you which model is *best*. That's deliberate. "Which one is best for me" is a genuine, answerable question — it's just a different one, and it gets its own honest module next, no hype and no crowned winner.

## The Automatos peek

Because no single model wins every job, Automatos doesn't lock you to one. In its Settings you can choose which model each of your AI workers uses — a small, fast, cheap one for simple replies, a big flagship for the hard thinking — and you can even bring your own account key from a maker like OpenAI or Anthropic so the work runs on your own arrangement with them. It can also fall back to another model automatically if your first choice is momentarily unavailable. You met the map in this module; Automatos is one place that lets you actually *use* all of it, mixing makers and sizes to fit each task — which is exactly the three-question filter, put to work.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You need a quick, routine reply drafted — nothing tricky. Which choice does the three-question filter point to, and why?

options.text: A small model — it's faster and cheaper, and it's plenty for a simple job; the big one would be overkill
The biggest flagship model — bigger is always better, whatever the task
Whichever model has the highest version number across all makers
None — you can't use AI for simple jobs, only for hard ones

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The first question is big-or-small, and a simple job doesn't need the big brain — a small model is faster, cheaper, and completely up to it, so (a) is right. "Bigger is always better" (b) is exactly the trap the module warns against: the flagship costs more and runs slower for no gain on easy work. Chasing the highest version number across makers (c) is the other trap — version numbers only compare a maker to its own past, not across companies. And AI is great for simple jobs (d) — that's precisely what the small models are for.

knowledgeCheck.stem: One maker's model is called "version 4" and a different maker's is "version 2." A friend says the 4 is obviously better because 4 is bigger than 2. What's the honest response?

options.text: Version numbers only compare a maker to its own past releases, so a "4" from one company tells you nothing about whether it beats a "2" from another
Your friend is right — a higher version number always beats a lower one, no matter which company made it
The lower number is always the better one, because newer isn't real
Version numbers are meaningless and tell you nothing at all, even within one maker

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Each company counts its own releases on its own private clock, so version numbers compare a maker only to its own past — one firm's "4" and another's "2" aren't on the same scale, making (a) correct. "Higher number always wins across makers" (b) is the exact trap the module names. Lower-is-better (c) is just as wrong — within a family, newer usually *is* an improvement. And the numbers aren't meaningless (d): within one maker they do signal "newer, usually better," which is a real (if narrow) use.

questions.stem: Which sentence best captures why there are so many AI model names?

options.text: A few companies each build their own family of models, each family comes in several sizes, and each keeps releasing newer versions
There's really only one AI, and the many names are just marketing for the same thing
Every website that uses AI invents a brand-new model of its own from scratch
The names are random and follow no pattern you could ever make sense of

questions.explanation: The list is makers × sizes × versions — several makers, a range of sizes each, and new versions over time — so (a) names all three sources. It isn't one AI rebranded (b): the different sizes and versions are genuinely different trained brains. Ordinary websites don't build their own models from scratch (c); they use models the big makers built. And the names aren't random (d) — the whole point of the module is that a tiny, learnable pattern generates the big list.

questions.stem: Match the maker to its model family. Which pairing is correct?

options.text: OpenAI makes GPT, Anthropic makes Claude, Google makes Gemini, and Meta makes Llama
Google makes GPT, and OpenAI makes Gemini
Meta makes Claude, and Anthropic makes Llama
All four families are made by the same single company

questions.explanation: The honest, neutral pairing is OpenAI/GPT, Anthropic/Claude, Google/Gemini, Meta/Llama — so (a) is correct. The others swap the makers around: Google doesn't make GPT and OpenAI doesn't make Gemini (b); Anthropic makes Claude, not Llama, and Meta makes Llama, not Claude (c). And they're not all one company (d) — separate makers building rival families is the first reason the list is long. Naming who makes what is just orientation; the module deliberately crowns no winner among them.

questions.stem: Within a single maker's family, what does a newer version number usually tell you?

options.text: It's generally an improvement on that maker's own older versions
It's guaranteed to beat every other maker's models too
It's slower and worse than the older version it replaced
It costs nothing to run, unlike the older versions

questions.explanation: Makers release newer versions because they've improved the brain, so within one family newer is usually better — (a). It is not guaranteed to beat rival makers (b): version numbers only compare a company to its own past, never across the fence. It isn't slower-and-worse (c) — that's backwards from the whole reason new versions ship. And a version number says nothing about price (d); cost tracks more with size (big versus small) than with the version number.

questions.stem: Between a maker's big flagship model and its smaller models, what's the honest trade-off?

options.text: The big one is the smartest but slower and pricier to run; the smaller ones are faster and cheaper and are plenty for simple jobs
The big one is better in every way and the small ones exist only to trick beginners
The small ones are always smarter; the big one is just more expensive for no reason
There's no difference between them apart from the name

questions.explanation: Bigger buys more capability but costs speed and money, while smaller trades a little capability for being fast, cheap, and entirely sufficient on easy work — so (a) is the honest trade-off. The big one isn't better in every way (b): its cost and slower speed are real downsides, and the small models are genuinely useful, not a trick. The small ones aren't *smarter* (c) — they're lighter; the flagship is the smarter one for hard problems. And they do differ (d) — size is a real difference, not just a label.

questions.stem: What are the three questions the module gives you for making sense of the model list?

options.text: Is it big or small, is it recent, and does it fit my job
What colour is the logo, how much does the company advertise, and is it popular
Which model do most experts personally prefer, and why
How many people use it, in how many countries, in how many languages

questions.explanation: The three-question filter is big-or-small (match size to the job), recent (newer within a family is usually better), and fit (does it suit the actual task) — so (a) is it. Logos and ad-spend and popularity (b) don't tell you whether a model fits your work. Expert preference (c) edges into "which is best," which is deliberately the next module, not this filter. And user counts across countries and languages (d) are trivia, not a way to choose — the filter is built to be simple and personal.

questions.stem: You picked a model for a task and it's underwhelming. According to the module, what's true about switching?

options.text: Choosing a model is a Tuesday decision, not a marriage — you can switch any time, even mid-task, with nothing locking you in
You've committed to that model now and have to stick with it for a long time
Switching models means starting your entire understanding of AI over from scratch
You should never switch, because trying a second model would be wasteful

questions.explanation: The module is explicit that picking a model is reversible — a Tuesday decision, not a marriage — so trying one never locks you in and (a) is correct. You are not committed for the long haul (b): that's the exact stress the module tells you to drop. Switching models doesn't reset your understanding (c) — the map (makers × sizes × versions) stays true whichever brain you use. And trying a second model isn't wasteful (d); it's the sensible response to a disappointing result.

questions.stem: In the car analogy the module uses, what do the manufacturers, the small-versus-big cars, and the yearly model updates each stand for?

options.text: Manufacturers are the makers, the small-versus-big cars are the model sizes, and the yearly updates are the new versions
Manufacturers are the versions, the cars are the makers, and the updates are the sizes
They all stand for the same thing — the analogy has only one part
The analogy is about fuel and mileage, which map to an AI's speed and cost

questions.explanation: The analogy lines up cleanly: manufacturers = makers, a city car versus an estate = small versus big model sizes, and new model years = new versions — so (a) is right. The mapping in (b) scrambles those roles. The analogy has three parts, not one (c) — that's the whole reason it mirrors makers × sizes × versions. And it isn't about fuel and mileage (d); its real punchline is that you never needed to know every model and trim to pick a car that fits your journey — same with AI.

questions.stem: This module names several makers but never says which model is best. Why is that the right call?

options.text: "Which is best for me" is a real, separate question with its own honest answer — it's the next module's job, so this one just gives you the map and stays neutral
Because there genuinely is one model that's best at everything, but it's a secret
Because comparing models is impossible and nobody should ever try
Because all models are identical, so a comparison would be pointless

questions.explanation: The module's job is the map and the filter; "which is best for me" is a real question that gets its own honest treatment next — so staying neutral here is deliberate, making (a) correct. There isn't one secret model that's best at everything (b): the module's core claim is that no single model wins every job. Comparing models isn't impossible (c) — it's exactly what the next module does, carefully. And models aren't identical (d): makers, sizes, and versions differ in real ways, which is why a considered comparison is worth doing later.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Models settings (choose the model per agent, bring your own key)
resources.annotation: The page behind this module's Automatos peek. It shows that Automatos treats models as swappable: you set a default model, each agent can use a different one, you add your own provider keys for makers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google and others, and a fallback chain can switch to another model automatically if your first choice is unavailable. Concrete proof of the module's point — no single model wins every job, so the platform lets you mix makers and sizes to fit the task.

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide (model families, in plain business terms)
resources.annotation: A vendor-light, beginner-toned primer. For this module, read only the model-families material: it backs the plain-English framing that different makers build different families of models, that models come in a range of sizes trading capability against speed and cost, and that newer versions keep arriving. Orientation for the makers × sizes × versions map, not a spec sheet — take the concepts, not any dated numbers.

resources.title: Hugging Face — Models hub (browse the real landscape of model families)
resources.annotation: A neutral, vendor-agnostic catalogue where the many model families and their different sizes and versions are all listed side by side in one place. Useful here just to *see* the makers × sizes × versions pattern is real out in the world — the list is long, and it's still only a few makers, a few sizes each, and versions over time. For orientation and a sense of scale, not for picking a winner.

videos.title: 02 · Why are there so many models? — the tiny pattern behind the huge list, in 7 minutes
02 · Deep Dive — makers, sizes, versions, and the three-question filter
